⏺ Both questions are now resolved. Here's the full picture.

  **1. Schema site — "0 tables" was a cache, now fixed**
     
  The corrected `metadata.json` (42 tables, all new datasets tagged) was live on GCS — I verified it by curl. The problem was purely caching: earlier today the
  buggy release run wrote a 30-table `metadata.json` to that exact URL, and GCS serves these sidecars with cache-control: public, max-age=3600, so your browser held
  the stale copy for an hour and masked the fixed re-upload.

  I patched it two ways:
  - Live sidecars (`versions.json`, this release's `metadata/catalog/relationships/erd`) → re-tagged cache-control: no-cache on GCS (verified at origin).
  - Future releases → `release_database.qmd` now tags those sidecars `no-cache` automatically, so a corrected re-render is never masked again.
  
  You need one hard refresh (Cmd+Shift+R) to drop the copy your browser cached earlier — after that it stays fresh on its own.

  **2. ERDDAP — git pull alone is not enough; it's a 2-part deploy**

  The datasets aren't showing because neither half has reached the server yet, and git push/pull only handles one half:
  
  ┌─────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │          Half           │                               Lives where                                │             How it reaches the server             │
  ├─────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Config (datasets.xml)   │ CalCOFI/erddap repo → host clone at /share/github/CalCOFI/erddap/content │ git — but still in OPEN PR #1, not merged to main │
  ├─────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Data (parquet, ~948 MB) │ /share/erddap/datasets/ (gitignored — not in any repo)                   │ must sync separately                              │
  └─────────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┴───────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  So yes-and-no: push/pull moves the config, but the parquet is gitignored and won't come with git pull. I just uploaded the 5 staged parquet to
  gs://calcofi-db/erddap/ so the server can pull from GCS (it already has calcofi-db rclone access) instead of a 948 MB uplink from your laptop. I also fixed a 
  bug that would've loaded the datasets empty — recursive=false with parquet nested one level deep — pushed to PR #1.

  Three steps on the host to make them appear at /erddap/info/index.html:

  ```bash
  # 1. config — after merging CalCOFI/erddap PR #1 to main:
  cd /share/github/CalCOFI/erddap && git pull
  
  # 2. data — pull the parquet from GCS into the datasets mount:
  rclone copy gcs-calcofi-sa:calcofi-db/erddap /share/erddap/datasets --progress
  
  # 3. reload — touch a flag per dataset (immediate; else ~hourly auto-reload):
  for id in calcofi_ctd calcofi_casts calcofi_dic calcofi_euphausiids calcofi_zooplankton; do
    touch /share/erddap/data/flag/$id
  done
  ```
  
  (I can't run these from here — it's the remote VM. If you SSH in, you can run them, or paste them via ! ssh … in this session.) One thing to verify: the live
  info page returned a 302 just now, so confirm the erddap container is actually healthy before/after.
  
  **3. datacheck app — built and working ✅**
  
  apps/datacheck/ is committed and pushed to CalCOFI/apps (main). It loads clean (HTTP 200, 987 cruises, 5.87M observations across 9 datasets, no boot errors).
  Run it with shiny::runApp("apps/datacheck").
  
  The headline feature ctd-viz lacks — URL deep-linking: pick a cruise → every dataset's stations on a map (colored per dataset) + table + space-time plot, and
  the whole view round-trips through the URL so you can hand someone a permalink for a provider question:
  
  ?cruise=1998-04-31JD&datasets=calcofi_ctd-cast,swfsc_ichthyo&id=calcofi_dic:1234
  
  1998-04-31JD is a good demo cruise — 7 datasets co-sampled. A Copy link button grabs the current view.
  
  Remaining for you: merge the 3 PRs (workflows#60, calcofi4db#2, erddap#1) and run the 3 ERDDAP host steps. Want me to add a prep_db.R + deploy note for
  datacheck to the server's app-provisioning, or move on to phytoplankton #28?

  bebest_ucsd_edu@shiny-server:/share/erddap/datasets$ ls                                                                                                         
  bebest_ucsd_edu@shiny-server:/share/erddap/datasets$ rclone copy gcs-calcofi-sa:calcofi-db/erddap /share/erddap/datasets --progress

⏺ That confirms it: the gcs-calcofi-sa remote lives inside the rclone container (base image rclone/rclone:latest, default config /config/rclone/rclone.conf —
  which is why the bare rclone sync in the cron script works without --config). Your host shell has no such config. And critically, the container mounts
  /share:/share, so writing to /share/erddap/datasets from inside the container lands on the host.
     
  Run the copy through the container:
     
  ```bash
  docker exec rclone rclone copy gcs-calcofi-sa:calcofi-db/erddap /share/erddap/datasets --progress
  ```
  
  Then verify the parquet landed (note the nested layout my recursive=true fix expects):
  
  ```bash
  find /share/erddap/datasets -name '*.parquet'
  # expect: /share/erddap/datasets/calcofi_ctd/ctd_wide/ctd_wide.parquet  (+ 4 small ones)
  ```
  
  Don't forget the config half (the data alone won't show datasets). The host clone at /share/github/CalCOFI/erddap needs the datasets.xml — that's still in open 
  PR #1. Either merge PR #1 then git pull, or pull the branch directly to test now:
  
  ```bash
  cd /share/github/CalCOFI/erddap && git fetch && git checkout add-calcofi-parquet-datasets && git pull
  grep -c '<recursive>true' content/datasets.xml   # should print 5
  ```
  
  Then reload (flag dir is /share/erddap/data/flag/ on the host → /erddapData/flag in the container):
  
  ```bash
  for id in calcofi_ctd calcofi_casts calcofi_dic calcofi_euphausiids calcofi_zooplankton; do
    touch /share/erddap/data/flag/$id
    # touch /share/erddap/data/flag/calcofi_phytoplankton
  done
  ```
  
  Give it ~1–2 min, then refresh https://erddap.calcofi.io/erddap/info/index.html — the 5 datasets should appear.
  
  Two things to watch:
  - If docker exec … rclone copy errors with a permission denied writing to /share/erddap/datasets, the container user can't write there — sudo chown -R it to
  match, or run the host-rclone variant: rclone --config /share/rclone/rclone.conf --gcs-service-account-file /etc/rclone/calcofi-admin-sa.json copy 
  gcs-calcofi-sa:calcofi-db/erddap /share/erddap/datasets --progress.
  - If a dataset still doesn't load after the flag, check /share/erddap/data/logs/log.txt for WARNING: Bad line(s) — that's usually a Parquet column-type mismatch
  in the XML.

  datacheck to the server's app-provisioning, then move on to phytoplankton #28


⏺ Recorded on #28. The data is acquired and the hard parsing is proven. Before I build the multi-table ingest, there's one schema decision that shapes everything
  — and since this project is fundamentally about schema, it's worth your call:
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
←  ☐ Phyto grain  ☐ How far  ✔ Submit  →

Phytoplankton is region-pooled (4 regions per cruise: NE/SE/Alley/Offshore), with NO per-station position — a real departure from every other dataset's 
per-station grain. How should I model it in the integrated schema?
     
❯ 1. Native region grain (Recommended)
     phyto_sample = one row per (cruise, region); link to cruise via cruise_key by year-month; region carries a centroid lat/lon + maps to its CalCOFI grid-cell 
     set; NO site_key. phyto_measurement long by taxon. Honest about pooling, still cross-referenceable by cruise/region on the map.
  2. Force a representative station
     Assign each region a representative line/station so it gets a site_key and joins like the others. Cleaner joins, but fabricates a precise position the pooled
     data doesn't actually have.
  3. Fully standalone
     Own tables only, no cruise_key/region linkage into the integrated schema. Simplest to ingest, but loses the cross-dataset value (won't appear in
     datacheck-by-cruise).


←  ☒ Phyto grain  ☐ How far  ✔ Submit  →

How far should I take phytoplankton tonight?

❯ 1. Full ingest + release (Recommended)
     Build the ingest notebook, WoRMS-standardize taxa, match cruise_key, run it, validate, export parquet, and roll into a new release — same end-to-end as the 
     other datasets.
  2. Ingest to parquet, hold release
     Build + run the ingest to parquet/GCS, but don't cut a new release until you review the region model and Q01 answers.
  3. Scaffold + stop for review
     Write the ingest notebook + metadata scaffolding but don't run it yet; wait for your review of the grain decision first.

What is after #28 ingest phytoplankton? 
     
⏺ Phytoplankton #28 is the last must-complete ingest. Everything after it is should-complete or nice-to-have, and all of it is currently gated on acquiring data 
  from CalCOFI's online databases (the same blocker I just cleared for #28 by finding it on EDI). From the tracker:
     
  ┌─────────────┬───────┬─────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │    Next     │ Issue │      Tier       │                              Status / blocker                               │
  ├─────────────┼───────┼─────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ ZooDb       │ #31   │ should-complete │ DataZoo online DB (holoplankton) — needs an export/API pull                 │
  ├─────────────┼───────┼─────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ ZooScan     │ #32   │ should-complete │ ZooScan online DB (imagery-based counts) — needs an export                  │
  ├─────────────┼───────┼─────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ PRODO       │ #34   │ nice-to-have    │ Primary production — needs a source + design decision                       │
  ├─────────────┼───────┼─────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ ~~Seabird~~ │ #29   │ should-complete │ Already covered by bird_mammal_census (ingested) — redundant row, closeable │
  ├─────────────┼───────┼─────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ ~~Mammals~~ │ #30   │ should-complete │ Already covered by bird_mammal_census — closeable                           │
  └─────────────┴───────┴─────────────────┴─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
  
  So the realistic sequence after #28:
  
  1. Finish #28 (in progress — you just approved the full ingest + release; I'll continue once you're ready).
  2. #31 ZooDb and #32 ZooScan — both are CalCOFI zooplankton online databases. I'd attack them like #28: probe EDI / DataZoo / the ZooScan portal for a

## 2026-06-05 schema updates

We have created this nice overall schema description page `../schema/` (rendered at https://calcofi.io/schema). Many of the tables have no relationships and there is no indication in the ERD of dataset source, whereas color coding exists in `@release_database.qmd`, but it seems hard-coded (rendered at https://calcofi.io/workflows/release_database.html#show-combined-schema). I would like to see relationships exist where possible, which means updating the associated `ingest_*.qmd` scripts.

I like the **Datasets** tab but would hope that it could also show us the tables each dataset contributes and if the table is mixed then something like # of rows and/or percentage contribution. Would be good to also include the ingest_* workflow(s)(linked to rendered html) used to generate the table and/or dataset.

As much as possible, I would like to use the yaml of the input `./ingest_*.qmd` and the sidecar files uploaded into GCS to efficiently track and store in an efficient, authoritative manner.

I like the pill tags added to **Tables** for provider and dataset, but they are not clickable, nor seemingly reused elsewhere (except cryptically in Datasets). It might be nice to be able to add a filter interface to click on one or more tags that filters for across Datasets, **Tables**, **Columns** and **Measurements**.

Planning: /Users/bbest/.claude/plans/typed-humming-crab.md

←  ☐ ERD coloring  ☐ Contribution detail  ☐ Sequencing  ☐ Source of truth  ✔ Submit  →
  
**ERD coloring** The ERD color coding in release_database.qmd is hard-coded into 7 manual groups (and ichthyo is split across 3 colors: cruise/ship/site/tow/net, ichthyo/species/lookup, grid/segment). To make it 'indicate 
dataset source' authoritatively, how should colors be derived?

  1. By dataset (provider_dataset)
     One color per dataset (e.g. swfsc_ichthyo, calcofi_bottle, calcofi_ctd-cast, calcofi_dic). Collapses ichthyo's 3 sub-colors into 1. Driven from table→dataset metadata, not a hard-coded list. Shared
     tables (measurement_type, dataset, _spatial) get a neutral color.
  2. By provider
     Coarser: one color per provider (calcofi, swfsc, pic...). Fewer colors but bottle/ctd/dic all share one.
  3. Keep manual groups, data-driven
     Preserve the current finer visual grouping but move the list out of hard-code into a metadata file so it's maintained in one place.
❯ 4. Let's color code by dataset (provider_dataset) and add an optional yaml config for the color to apply along with exceptions to apply on a table by table basis for anything unique. This could also apply to the release_database.qmd which would apply an override, especially for common tables. And rather than using the `fill` which applies to alternating rows like in https://calcofi.io/workflows/release_database.html#show-combined-schema, let's use something subtler and less busy like `stroke`.

**Contribution detail** For 'mixed' tables (e.g. measurement_type, ship, dataset — contributed/modified
by multiple ingests), how much contribution detail do you want on the Datasets
tab?

❯ 1. Row counts + % per dataset
     Each ingest records how many rows it contributes to each table; Datasets
     tab shows e.g. 'measurement_type: 42 rows (39%) from calcofi_bottle'. Most
     informative but requires instrumenting each ingest_*.qmd to emit
     per-table row counts.
  2. List contributors only
     Show which datasets/workflows contribute to each table (and link the
     workflow), but no row counts/percentages. Much cheaper — derivable from
     existing YAML + dataset.csv 'tables' column.
  3. Total rows + contributor list
     Show the table's total row count (already in catalog.json) plus the list
     of contributing datasets/workflows, but skip per-dataset row splitting.

**Sequencing** This spans 3 layers: the schema/ frontend (cheap, no pipeline re-run), the 
workflows backend (ingest FK definitions + metadata, requires re-running 
release_database.qmd), and R packages (erd.R/wrangle.R). How should I sequence 
the plan?

❯ 1. Full plan, all layers
     Plan everything end-to-end: backend relationship/metadata additions, 
     R-package ERD coloring, AND the frontend filter/Datasets/clickable-tags 
     work. One comprehensive plan.
  2. Frontend-first, backend follow-up
     Phase 1 = schema/ site changes that work against current data (clickable 
     tags, cross-tab filter, use existing relationships.json/catalog.json). 
     Phase 2 = backend FK + metadata enrichment. Lets you ship UI quickly.
  3. Backend-first
     Phase 1 = enrich relationships + metadata sidecars (so ERD draws more 
     edges, contributions exist). Phase 2 = frontend consumes the richer data.

**Source of truth** You want to use the ingest_*.qmd YAML as the authoritative source. Today the 
'calcofi:' YAML block has target_name/workflow_type/dependency/output/modifies,
while metadata/dataset.csv has the 'tables' (semicolon list) + citation 
columns. What should be authoritative for table→dataset→workflow mapping?

  1. Extend YAML, keep dataset.csv for citations
     Add provider/dataset/tables_owned to each ingest YAML 'calcofi:' block 
     (authoritative for which workflow produces which tables + the 
     rendered-HTML link). dataset.csv keeps citation/coverage/license. 
     release_database.qmd reads YAMLs to build the lineage sidecar.
  2. Keep dataset.csv authoritative
     Leave the table list in dataset.csv; just add a workflow/target_name 
     column there. Don't touch YAML beyond what exists.
❯ 3. YAML authoritative for everything
     Move dataset-level metadata into YAML too, deprecating dataset.csv. Larger
     refactor.

⏺ All five layers are implemented, verified, and recorded. Here's the summary.

  **What I built**
  
  I implemented the full plan across three repos + two R packages, all driven authoritatively
  from each ingest's YAML and the GCS sidecars.
  
  **1. YAML authoritative (deprecates `metadata/dataset.csv`)**
  
  Each `ingest_*.qmd` `calcofi:` block now carries `provider`, `dataset`, `workflow_url`, `dataset_meta`
  (citation/coverage/license/links), `tables_owned`, and `erd.color`. ichthyo folds `swfsc_invert` via
  `additional_datasets:`. New `calcofi4db` functions read these: `read_ingest_yaml()`,
  `read_calcofi_meta()`, `ingest_yaml_to_dataset_df()` (rebuilds the in-DB dataset registry — ingests
  no longer read the CSV). Setup chunks now pull `provider`/`dataset` from their own YAML.

  **2. ERD relationships + dataset coloring**
  
  - `cc_erd()` switched to stroke-only styling (no busy alternating fill); new `cc_erd_color_map()`
  derives one color per dataset from table→dataset metadata, shared/common tables → neutral, with
  overrides.
  - **Key fix**: the `erd.mmd` uploaded to GCS previously passed no colors — the schema-site ERD has
  been colorless. It now gets the color map.
  - Added safe FK edges (verified against real columns): ctd→measurement_type; ichthyo
  grid/segment PKs, segment→cruise/site, invert→net/species; release `cross_fks` for
  ctd_cast→cruise/ship/grid and dic_sample→casts/bottle.
  
  **3. Contributions + workflow links**
  
  `merge_metadata_json` now emits `metadata.json` v1.2 with per-table workflow, a contributions block
  (per-dataset rows + %, over_attributed flag for deduped shared tables), `erd_legend`,
  `datasets[].tables`, and `measurement_types[].datasets`. Each ingest counts its own rows per
  `tables_owned` (reference tables like grid correctly excluded).

  **4. Frontend (clickable tags + cross-tab filter)**
  
  `schema/` gets a global dataset filter bar, clickable chips in Tables/Datasets, and one delegated
  handler that filters Tables/Columns/Measurements/Datasets together (OR union, composes with
  text search, persists across tab switches). The Datasets tab shows each dataset's contributed
  tables with rows/% and workflow ↗ links. ERD legend added. Everything reads new fields
  defensively so old releases still render.

  **Verified**

  - Both R packages load; new functions exported; docs generated.
  - cc_erd_color_map + cc_erd produce stroke-only classDefs (no fill), correct dataset colors,
  shared/common tables neutral.
  - Full metadata.json contract from R matches what app.js reads (contributions %,
  over_attributed, per-table workflow, dataset tables, measurement datasets, erd_legend).
  - build_metadata_json contributions: owned-only, correct counts/shared flags, missing-table
  warning.
  - app.js passes node --check.

  **To deploy (requires you to run)**
  
  1. Reinstall/load_all `calcofi4db` + `calcofi4r`.
  2. Re-run all 4 `ingest_*.qmd`, then `release_database.qmd` → regenerates GCS sidecars.
  3. Deploy the `schema/` site (CalCOFI/schema GitHub Pages).

  I couldn't run a browser here, so the one thing left to eyeball is the live UI — the plan's
  verification section covers it: `jekyll serve schema/` with `SCHEMA_GCS_BASE` pointed at the new
  release, then check the stroke-colored ERD + legend, chip filtering across tabs, the Datasets
  rows/%/links, and that an older version still renders.

  One open item I flagged: swfsc_invert row-level contribution isn't attributed (invert is folded
  into ichthyo's DB) — its Datasets card lists the invert table from the registry but without a
  row count/%. Let me know if you want that wired up.

what's the status -- has been a while

  picture:

  headline CTD table is ctd_thin at ~258 MB).
  - The CTD ingest re-ran, so all 96 partitions are being fully re-uploaded to GCS at ~0.5 MiB/s.
  Only 15/96 done (~16%, currently at 2005-11).
  - At this rate the CTD→GCS leg alone is likely another ~2 hours; after that, dic +
  release_database + test_release are quick (minutes).

  The key insight: my changes don't alter ctd_measurement's data — only metadata
  (rels/tables_owned/YAML). The 15 GB re-upload is incidental; the schema-site features actually
  come from the small metadata.json / erd.mmd / relationships.json / contributions that

✻ Cogitated for 2m 31s · 1 shell still running

If only the sidecar files for the ctd-cast are changed, why then should all the parquet files  
  need re-uploading? This is a fundamental glitch that will come back to haunt on every change   
  -- we need to streamline this so only actually changed files are uploaded to GCS, otherwise    
  used from previous                                                                             
  
⏺ Good catch — that's the right thing to fix at the root. Re-uploading 15 GB of byte-identical
  data on every metadata-only change is wasteful. Let me investigate the actual upload path
  (read-only; won't disturb the running pipeline) to find why it re-sends everything.

⏺ This is the root cause: stable historical partitions (2012, 2014, 2016) all have different
  crc32c and different sizes between the freshly regenerated local parquet and the prior GCS
  version — for data that hasn't changed. So re-generating parquet is byte-non-deterministic
  (almost certainly nondeterministic row ordering → different compression). The crc32c sync would
  re-upload them even if its hash command worked — and separately, sync_to_gcs's gcloud storage
  hash --crc32c is broken on gcloud 563, silently falling back to size.

  Let me verify the determinism hypothesis and read how parquet is written, so the fix is
  grounded:

⏺ Determinism test confirms the fix direction: ORDER BY makes parquet byte-identical run-to-run
  (ordered c==e: TRUE), while large unordered parallel writes drift. So there are two real
  defects:

  1. Non-deterministic parquet ordering — large partitioned tables (like ctd_measurement) are
  written without a stable ORDER BY, so every re-run produces different bytes → crc32c/size
  differ → full re-upload.
  2. Broken hash command — sync_to_gcs calls gcloud storage hash --crc32c which gcloud 563
  rejects, silently falling back to size-only comparison.

  Let me see how the CTD ingest calls write_parquet_outputs (does it pass sort_by for the big
  tables?) and the order-clause logic:

⏺ Found the precise root cause. ctd_measurement does have sort_by = c("measurement_type", 
  "depth_m") — but that's not a unique ordering. Many rows share the same (cruise_key,
  measurement_type, depth_m), and those ties break nondeterministically across parallel runs →
  different bytes every time. Same issue for bottle_measurement (sorted by measurement_type
  only). The fix is a deterministic tiebreaker (the primary key) appended to the sort, plus
  fixing the hash command.
  
  Let me confirm the corrected hash command and that a unique tiebreaker yields byte-stable
  output:

⏺ Both confirmed: the corrected command gcloud storage hash <path> emits crc32c_hash: (and it
  matches the earlier local hash), and a unique tiebreaker guarantees byte-identical output. The
  fix is well-grounded. Let me check which ingests call write_parquet_outputs so I update them
  all:

⏺ Now write_parquet_outputs — add primary_keys param and the deterministic tiebreaker. Let me
  view the signature and roxygen:

Let's also implement these default setting recommendations from DuckLake engineers [Image #1]

⏺ Good additions — those DuckLake/parquet settings are deterministic (they won't conflict with
  the byte-stability fix) and pair naturally with this work. Let me fold them in. First let me
  finish the tiebreaker edit and apply the parquet settings (V2, zstd, 80MB row groups) to the
  COPY statements. Let me read the current write section:

⏺ Important: partitioned writes with ROW_GROUP_SIZE_BYTES (which forces
  preserve_insertion_order=false) are not deterministic. Determinism is the higher priority (it's
  what stops the re-uploads). Let me find the combination that's both deterministic and uses the
  recommended settings for partitioned tables:

Prioritize insertion order over row group size for the sake of deterministically avoiding      
  re-upload of same data

⏺ Agreed — determinism wins. But my last test showed partitioned output is non-deterministic even
  with preserve_insertion_order=true + ORDER BY unless an explicit row-group size is set. Let me
  rigorously confirm the reliable combination (3 writes each) before committing:

⏺ Key finding: threads=1 makes partitioned writes reliably deterministic, and an
  order-independent content hash works (even for geometry via ST_AsWKB). But before choosing, let
  me settle whether full-thread writes with preserve_insertion_order=true + unique ORDER BY and
  no row-group override are reliably deterministic (your stated preference). Definitive 5× test:

⏺ Definitive result: full-thread partitioned writes are not deterministic (2 distinct
  byte-outputs across 5 runs) even with preserve_insertion_order=true and a unique ORDER BY. The
  only ways to get byte-stable partitioned parquet are threads=1 (deterministic but slow) or not 
  rewriting unchanged data at all.

  This changes the implementation choice, so I want to confirm before building the larger piece:
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
←  ☐ Dedup approach  ☐ Parquet settings  ✔ Submit  →
  
**Dedup approach** Partitioned parquet can't be made byte-deterministic at full threads (DuckDB parallel partition 
writer). Both options below stop re-uploading unchanged data — they differ in whether we also 
avoid the expensive re-write. Which approach?
  
❯ 1. Content-hash skip (recommended)
     Compute an order-independent content signature per table; store it in manifest.json. On
     re-run, if a table's data is unchanged AND its local parquet still exists, skip BOTH
     re-writing and re-uploading — reuse the previous files. Fastest (no 15 GB re-write on
     metadata-only changes), robust regardless of write nondeterminism. Most code. Matches 'used
     from previous' exactly.
  2. threads=1 deterministic write
     Force single-threaded ordered writes (preserve_insertion_order=true + unique ORDER BY + PK
     tiebreaker) so identical data yields identical bytes; the crc32c sync then skips upload.
     Simpler, but re-writes the full 15 GB single-threaded on EVERY ingest re-run (slow), even
     for metadata-only changes — only the upload is skipped.
  3. Both: hash-skip + threads=1 fallback
     Content-hash skip as primary; when a table IS rewritten, also use threads=1 so its bytes are
     stable. Maximum robustness, most complexity.

**Parquet settings**  Apply the DuckLake engineers' parquet quality settings where they don't conflict with the
   dedup goal? 
   → I think `V2 + zstd, skip row-group-bytes`, but want to confirm that we retain the native 
   GEOMETRY introduced in recent DuckDB version 1.5+. Not having the GEOMETRY properly read and 
   write has been a huge headache in the past

⏺ GEOMETRY is fully preserved through V2 + zstd — round-trip returns GEOMETRY('OGC:CRS84'), ST_X
  works, all 1000 geoms identical. Your concern is addressed. (Pipeline: ichthyo + spatial done,
  ctd still uploading.)

  Now let me implement content-hash skip. Let me read the manifest-assembly section of
  write_parquet_outputs:

⏺ There's already a coarse skip — but for partitioned tables it's all-or-nothing: when new
  partition values appear (e.g. new 2026 cruises in the source), it forces a full re-write of all
  96 partitions (line 1415-1419). That's the real culprit — new data triggers
  re-writing/re-uploading unchanged historical partitions. The robust fix is per-partition 
  content-hash skip. Let me read the rest of the function to integrate it:

Given that the default compression is now changed (snappy to zstd) as is parquet version (1 to 
  2), I would expect that we would have to rewrite all parquet files, but I could be wrong with  
  new dedup strategy                                                                             
  
⏺ Excellent catch — you're exactly right. The content-hash compares logical data, not file bytes,
  so changing snappy→zstd / V1→V2 would not trigger a rewrite — the old-format files would
  persist forever. The dedup needs to also detect a format change and force a one-time rewrite.
  Let me add a format gate.

⏺ Wait — critical issue I need to verify: ctd/bottle use strip_provenance=FALSE, so the exported
  parquet (and thus my hash's exclude_cols) includes provenance columns like _ingested_at, which
  changes every run. That would make the content hash always differ → dedup never triggers for
  exactly the big tables we care about. The content hash must ignore provenance regardless of
  strip_provenance. Let me check and fix:

⏺ The big ctd_measurement table doesn't carry provenance columns, so it's fine — but other tables
  (e.g. invert) do carry _ingested_at, which changes every run and would defeat dedup. The
  correct, robust fix is to always exclude provenance columns from the content hash (dedup should
  track real data, not ingestion timestamps):

Also reset the working DuckLake (gs://calcofi-db/ducklake/working/calcofi.duckdb) for a 
from-scratch wrangle?

❯ 1. Keep it (recommended)
     Leave the working DuckLake; ingests overwrite their tables in it. The new parquet settings 
     apply at export time, not in the DuckLake, so a reset isn't needed.
  2. Reset it
     Delete the working DuckLake so ingests rebuild every table from source. Slower, fully 
     from-scratch. Only needed if you suspect stale state in the wrangle DB.

   duckdb on GCS

⏺ Confirmed: release_version <- format(Sys.Date(), "v%Y.%m.%d") → today's run is v2026.06.06
  automatically. Here's the answer to your working-DuckLake question:

  Two different things, often conflated:

  1. Admin "Working DuckLake" (calcofi4db::get_working_ducklake()) — despite the name, it's a
  plain DuckDB file, not a true DuckLake (no ATTACH … TYPE ducklake anywhere). If used,
  finalize_ingest() materializes parquet into it (CREATE TABLE AS SELECT * FROM read_parquet(…)),
  which would duplicate the data inside the .duckdb. But in the current pipeline it's vestigial:
  (a) none of the 4 ingests call finalize_ingest, (b) gs://calcofi-db/ducklake/working/ is empty
  (no object there), and (c) release_database doesn't read it — it builds an in-memory DuckDB
  with VIEWs over local parquet. So there's nothing to reset.
  2. User-facing DB (calcofi4r::cc_get_db()) — exactly as you remembered: default
  local_data=FALSE makes remote VIEWs over released parquet at …/releases/{version}/parquet via
  httpfs (no download, no duplication); local_data=TRUE downloads. It reads releases, never the
  working DuckLake.
  
  So "reset working DuckLake" is moot — nothing exists in GCS to reset, and it's off the
  release/user path.

  GCS state now: ingest/ fully deleted; releases intact (v2026.05.20 still latest); working dir
  empty.

  Now the clean re-run — invalidate all targets (so ichthyo/spatial re-run with the new code, not
  skipped) and relaunch:

  ingests, release_database, or calcofi4r — nothing to reset. cc_get_db() uses remote parquet

⏺ The run is progressing and the detector is armed (it'll ping me on completion, error, or a
  20-min hang). Here's what was wrong and what I fixed this turn:

  1. The 12-hour "stall" = ichthyo's quarto render wedged after knitting (a deno output-pipe
  deadlock, 0% CPU). Transient — it didn't recur (ichthyo completed in 3 min on retry).
  2. ctd error #1 = a stale DuckDB WAL left by killing the render mid-write (WAL checkpoint 
  iteration mismatch). Fixed: cleared the stale .wal/.tmp, and added defensive WAL/tmp cleanup to
  all four ingests' overwrite blocks so a future kill won't wedge them.
  3. ctd error #2 = a latent bug in ctd's eval-toggle logic: on the checkpoint-restore path the
  chunk that re-enables computation (restore_eval) was itself being skipped, so
  write_parquet_outputs never ran but the downstream gantt_chart chunk read the never-written
  parquet. Fixed by giving restore_eval #| eval: true.
  4. Bonus dedup fix: all four ingests were deleting dir_parquet on every overwrite, which would
  wipe the prior manifest and defeat the content-hash dedup. Now they preserve dir_parquet
  (deletion moved to overwrite_all), so future runs actually skip unchanged partitions.

  ctd is now on the clean full-rebuild path (reading source → transform → write 15 GB zstd/V2 →
  upload), then bottle → dic → release → test. This is the slow leg (full source read + 15 GB
  write/upload), so expect a while.
  
  Note: these ingest .qmd fixes are uncommitted in the workflows repo (you'd asked me to
  bump/push only the R packages). I'll surface them for commit once the run succeeds and the
  schema site is deployed. I'll report back when the detector fires.

⏺ The ctd_wide upload (gcloud PID 15732) is wedged — STAT SN, 0% CPU, only 1.3s CPU in 25 min:
  another stalled large transfer. The good news: ctd_measurement/ctd_thin/ctd_summary (12.66 GB)
  already uploaded successfully. Let me look at the image you shared and check put_gcs_file's
  retry behavior before unsticking:

⏺ That speed test is the key — your upload is only 1.69 Mbps (~0.21 MB/s, server Turin). That
  reframes everything: the "stall" is almost certainly just a slow upload, not a hang — gcloud
  suppresses progress output to a redirected (non-TTY) log, so a slow transfer looks idle. Let me
  confirm ctd_wide is actively uploading (live connection) vs truly wedged:

⏺ The connections are in CLOSE_WAIT — Google closed them (the slow 1.69 Mbps trickle got dropped,
  very likely when the machine slept overnight) and gcloud is wedged instead of retrying. So
  it's genuinely stuck, not progressing. The 12.66 GB already uploaded is fine; only ctd_wide +
  measurement_type remain.

  Two fixes: kill the wedged upload and resume (dedup + crc-skip means it only retries the
  un-uploaded files), and run under caffeinate so overnight sleep can't drop the connection
  again. Let me get the process tree and kill it:

⏺ Clean kill; the 12.66 GB already uploaded is intact. Now relaunching under caffeinate so the
  machine can't sleep mid-upload (the likely cause of the dropped/CLOSE_WAIT connection). The
  re-run will dedup-reuse all ctd parquet (no rewrite) and crc-skip the 12.66 GB already in GCS —
  so it only resumes ctd_wide + measurement_type, then proceeds to bottle/dic/release:

⏺ The eval fix worked — ctd is now running the transforms (chunk 42/105, rebuilding tables) past
  restore_eval. It'll dedup-reuse the parquet and resume uploading ctd_wide. Let me arm a smarter
  detector that distinguishes a slow upload (ESTABLISHED connection) from a CLOSE_WAIT hang, so
  I catch a real wedge in ~3 min without false alarms on slow transfers:

⏺ Detector armed — it now distinguishes a slow upload (live ESTABLISHED connection) from a
  CLOSE_WAIT wedge, so a real hang is caught in ~3 min while slow transfers run undisturbed.

  Root cause of the overnight failure: your 1.69 Mbps upload + the machine sleeping overnight
  dropped the in-flight ctd_wide upload; Google closed the sockets (CLOSE_WAIT) and gcloud wedged
  instead of retrying. The earlier ichthyo deno hang was likely the same sleep-induced pipe
  break.

  What I changed:
  - Running the pipeline under caffeinate -i so it can't idle-sleep mid-transfer.
  - Resuming smartly: ctd's parquet is dedup-reused (no 15 GB rewrite) and the 12.66 GB already 
  in GCS is crc-skipped — so it only re-uploads ctd_wide (~960 MB, ~75 min at this uplink) + the
  tiny measurement_type, then continues to bottle → dic → release. 
  - release_database copies ingest→release server-side in GCS (no re-upload), so the release step
  won't be bandwidth-bound. 
  
  This run will still be slow purely due to the 1.69 Mbps uplink for the remaining ingest parquet
  — but that's exactly why the dedup work matters: once this baseline is uploaded, future runs
  send only changed partitions. I'll report the moment the detector fires (completion, error, or
  a real CLOSE_WAIT hang).

❯ I want the mermaid format to remain PNG so it can be zoomed in on via lightbox. Instead of 
  preventing headless Chrome from running to get the screenshot, can you make it work and not 
  hang? Here's some advice I see from Googling:

When Quarto hangs during headless Google Chrome processes (usually while rendering dynamic content like Mermaid/Graphviz diagrams or exporting to PDF/DOCX), it is frequently caused by a compatibility mismatch between modern Chrome updates and older Quarto versions, or orphaned zombie browser instances.

To quickly resolve this, try the following solutions:1. Force the New Headless ModeRecent Google Chrome updates removed the legacy headless mode, which can cause Quarto to error out or freeze. You can force Quarto to use the updated headless API by setting an environment variable before running your command.

On macOS/Linux in your terminal:

```bash
export QUARTO_CHROMIUM_HEADLESS_MODE=new
quarto render your_document.qmd
```
⏺ Good call — that's the proper fix. Chrome 147 (which you have) removed the legacy --headless 
  mode, and the earlier process showed Quarto launching Google Chrome --headless (legacy) →
  freeze. Forcing QUARTO_CHROMIUM_HEADLESS_MODE=new makes Quarto use the modern headless API. Let
  me revert to PNG and apply the env var persistently.

⏺ Now make the env var persistent in _targets.R so every pipeline run uses the modern headless
  API:

⏺ Update(_targets.R)
  ⎿  Added 7 lines
      14  library(targets)
      16  library(calcofi4db)
      17 +                                                                                
      18 +# Chrome >= 132 removed the legacy --headless mode that Quarto uses to          
      19 +# screenshot mermaid diagrams (mermaid-format: png), causing renders to hang.   
      20 +# Force the modern headless API so PNG mermaid (zoomable via lightbox) works. 
      21 +# Inherited by the quarto/Chrome subprocesses spawned via quarto_render(). 
      22 +Sys.setenv(QUARTO_CHROMIUM_HEADLESS_MODE = "new")
      23 + 
      24  build_targets_list(
      26    exclude = c("publish_ichthyo_to_obis")

  fails the release if it won't parse — and I documented keeping the site's mermaid_version ≥ the
  validating mmdc, so "valid here" always means "renders there."

## 2026-06-04 ca-ocean-monitoring-map

Help me refactor the code to consistently reference input files from the following data folder, particularly under `@./R/`:

`@~/My Drive/projects/calcofi/data-public/_projects/ca-ocean-monitoring-map/`

Use `here::here()` and `glue::glue()` where applicable for consistent, efficient file path naming.

Run the necessary R scripts to successful completion under under `@./R/` and test live in a web browser in  `@./web/index.html`, fixing any paths as needed. Provide instructions for hosting on our Google VM server or on Github Pages if possible. We could also use reference data files hosted on Google Cloud Service.

⏺ I've now mapped the situation. Before finalizing a plan, I need to surface a few things I found, because they block "run to completion" and only you can resolve them.
  
  What I found:
  - R scripts (R/): build_program_layer.R already uses here()/glue() (anchored to the repo via the .Rproj), but points at here("data") / here("outputs") — neither folder exists in the repo.
  build_combine_map.R and build_discharger_layer.R still hardcode Windows paths (C:/Users/bhuan/...).
  - The data folder (~/My Drive/.../ca-ocean-monitoring-map/) contains only raw input CSVs in 9 category folders (Carbonate Chemistry, eDNA, Fish Eggs and Larvae, Marine Mammals, Phytoplankton…, Primary
  Productivity, Seabirds, Standard Parameter, Zooplankton).
  - Missing reference files — nowhere on this machine: Attribute_Table.csv (read unconditionally at build_program_layer.R:81 — hard fail without it), ca_state/CA_State.shp (coastal clip), and the specific
  gebco_2025_...tif (depth; has a file.exists guard so it can degrade).
  - No pre-built outputs exist (Master_Inventory.geojson, transects.csv, Dischargers.geojson), so the web app currently has nothing to load.
  - Tooling is fine: R 4.5.2 with sf, terra, tidyverse, here, glue all installed; python3 available to serve web/.
  - Structural mismatch: build_program_layer.R processes one program folder → one layer (and was written for specific programs like CalCOFI/NOAA THL/SCCWRP per the README), but your data is organized by
  category, each with multiple program CSVs of differing schemas.
  
  Let me confirm how you want to handle the blockers:
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Planning: /Users/bbest/.claude/plans/help-me-refactor-the-generic-garden.md
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
←  ☐ Ref files  ☐ Layer mapping  ☐ Hosting  ✔ Submit  →

The pipeline needs 3 reference files that aren't in the data folder or anywhere on this Mac: Attribute_Table.csv (required — script hard-fails without it), CA_State.shp (coastal clip), and the 
gebco_2025_*.tif (depth). How should I proceed?

❯ 1. I'll add them to data folder
     You'll place Attribute_Table.csv, a ca_state/ shapefile, and the GEBCO .tif under the data folder, then I run the full pipeline against them.
  2. Make optional, run partial
     I refactor the scripts so missing reference files degrade gracefully (skip clip/depth/attribute enrichment) and run with whatever's available — output quality reduced.
  3. Refactor paths only, don't run yet
     I do the path refactor + hosting docs now; you supply reference files later and run the pipeline yourself.

←  ☒ Ref files  ☐ Layer mapping  ☐ Hosting  ✔ Submit  →

build_program_layer.R produces one map layer per program folder. Your data has 9 category folders. How should I map them to layers?

  1. One layer per category folder
     Run the program builder once per category folder (Carbonate Chemistry, eDNA, …), naming each layer after the folder.
  2. Start with one folder to validate
     Wire it up and run on a single small folder (e.g. eDNA) end-to-end first, then scale to the rest once it works.
❯ 3. Not sure — depends on Attribute_Table
     The schema mapping likely needs the Attribute_Table; decide after seeing whether the CSVs parse.

←  ☒ Ref files  ☒ Layer mapping  ☐ Hosting  ✔ Submit  →

Where should the final map + data be hosted?

  1. [ ] GitHub Pages
  Static site from the repo (web/). Simplest; data files must be small enough to commit (note: Standard Parameter raw CSV is 266 MB, but only built GeoJSON outputs get served).
  2. [ ] Google VM server
  Serve web/ + outputs from your existing Google Compute Engine VM (e.g. nginx/Apache or a simple static server).
❯ 3. [ ] Data on Google Cloud Storage
  Host the large GeoJSON/CSV outputs in a public GCS bucket and have the web app fetch them from there (works with either site host).

⏺ Everything is complete and verified. The locally-staged data files are correctly gitignored (they don't appear as
  untracked), and R/paths.R is the one new source file.

  Summary

  I refactored the pipeline to a single, portable path convention, ran it end-to-end against your data folder, and verified
  the map live in a browser.

  Path refactor (here() + glue())

  - New R/paths.R — single source of truth, sourced by all three scripts. Defines dir_data (the shared Drive folder,
  overridable via CALCOFI_DATA env var), dir_out/dir_tmp/dir_web via here(), and the three reference-file paths via glue().
  - build_combine_map.R and build_discharger_layer.R — removed the hardcoded C:/Users/bhuan/... Windows paths; both now
  source paths.R. The discharger script skips cleanly when no Dischargers/ folder exists.
  - build_program_layer.R — replaced the here("data")/here("outputs") block with paths.R; the input folder is now selected
  via a PROGRAM env var.

  Bugs found and fixed while making it actually run

  1. Attribute table schema — the real Attribute_Table.csv uses ACRYNOM (and split SAMPLING FREQUENCY/SAMPLING PLATFORM)
  columns; added a resilient normalize_attr_cols() so it maps to what the script expects.
  2. Pre-existing gebco_mean_depth bug — the column was renamed then re-selected by its old name, crashing every GeoJSON
  write. Fixed the duplicate.
  3. Transect header mismatch — the pipeline writes Latitude Start but the web app only looked for Latitude Start 
  (º)/latitude_start; added the actual names as fallbacks (so all 1,629 transects now draw).

  Layer labels (your choice)
  
  Added a theme_titles map so the 9 parameter-theme folders get friendly names (e.g. eDNA → "Environmental DNA (eDNA)") since
  they aren't programs in the attribute table.

  Pipeline run — all green

  All 9 theme folders built → combined into outputs/Master_Inventory.geojson (7,778 hexes, GEBCO depths) +
  outputs/transects.csv (1,645 rows).

  Web verification (headless Chrome screenshots)

  - Hex layer renders for all 9 themes with the legend and friendly labels.
  - Survey transects render offshore once toggled (validated my fix: 0 missing coords, 1,629 drawable).

  Hosting (web/index.html now has a DATA_BASE constant)

  - Data → GCS: gsutil mb + public read + CORS (required for cross-origin fetch) + gsutil rsync outputs/ gs://…, then set
  DATA_BASE = 'https://storage.googleapis.com/<bucket>/'. Full commands are in the README.
  - Site → GitHub Pages or your Google VM: serve web/ (only index.html is committed); data comes from the bucket via
  DATA_BASE.

  The full how-to-run and hosting steps are written into README.md, and outputs/ plus the locally-staged web/ data copies are
  gitignored. Nothing was committed — let me know if you'd like me to commit these changes.

  One thing to flag for later: the data is organized by parameter theme, but Attribute_Table.csv is program-centric (62
  acronyms), so Platform/Full Program Name show "Unknown" for these theme layers. If you later reorganize inputs by program
  (matching the acronyms), the same pipeline will populate that metadata automatically.

## 2026-05-20.b query bump with db release like schema

I'm confused how @../query/ is supposed to pick up the latest db version. Online at https://calcofi.io/query/ it is stuck at v2026.05.14, but we already have v2026.05.19 which is the default at             
  https://calcofi.io/schema/ (ie @../query/)  

⏺ I've got the picture now. Two-stage promotion, plus a poller in the query repo — and one of those stages is stalled.

  Chain:
  1. workflows/release_database.qmd uploads parquet + sidecars to gs://calcofi-db/ducklake/releases/{v}/ but explicitly does not touch latest.txt (line 812: "promotion deferred to test_release.qmd").
  2. workflows/test_release.qmd (added in ac5fde2, never run — not in _targets/meta/meta) runs the _queries/*.md suite against the new release; on full pass it writes latest.txt.
  3. query/.github/workflows/bump-default-version.yml polls latest.txt every 6h and bumps _config.yml:default_version, which triggers a Pages rebuild.

  Current state:
  - gs://.../latest.txt is already v2026.05.19 (yesterday's tar_make() ran an older release_database.qmd that did push latest.txt, before ac5fde2 introduced the deferral).
  - query/_config.yml:default_version is v2026.05.14 — never bumped.
  - bump-default-version.yml was added to main in commit e1b90e3 (also yesterday).
  
  So the gate (test_release.qmd) hasn't run yet for v2026.05.19, but it doesn't need to: latest.txt is already pointing where you want. The actual stall is just that
  query/.github/workflows/bump-default-version.yml hasn't fired since it was added — */6h cron + GitHub's discovery latency on newly-added workflows.

  Want me to:
  1. Trigger the bump workflow manually (gh workflow run in query/) so the site updates now, and/or
  2. Run tar_make("test_release") to formally pass the gate for v2026.05.19 (no-op for latest.txt since it's already pointing there, but registers the target so the next release goes through the proper gate)?

Yes, run the `tar_make("test_release")` b/c I am already seeing queries that do not run on @../query/, eg https://calcofi.io/query/#browse--cruises 
  ✗ Query failed: Binder Error: Referenced column "cruise_key" not found in FROM clause! Candidate bindings: "read_parquet.cruise_key_0", "read_parquet.site_key", "read_parquet.cast_key",
  "read_parquet.grid_key", "read_parquet._source_file" LINE 10: GROUP BY cruise_key ^ 

⏺ Heads-up: release_database got re-dispatched (targets thinks it's outdated, likely because release_database.qmd changed in ac5fde2). That'll generate a fresh v2026.05.20 release first, then test_release
  will run against it. Net effect: new release uploaded (~36 GB through GCS) and latest.txt promotion goes through the proper test gate.

  If you'd rather skip the re-upload and just test against the existing v2026.05.19, say the word and I'll cancel and run test_release.qmd directly against that pinned version. Otherwise I'll let it run.

⏺ All green.

  Final state:
  - gs://calcofi-db/ducklake/releases/latest.txt → v2026.05.20
  - test_results.json for v2026.05.20: 9 pass, 4 skip (JS-only bio-env queries), 0 fail
  - Schema browser at https://calcofi.io/schema/ will show v2026.05.20 as default
  
  Two PR branches pushed:
  
  - workflows fix-cruise-key-and-mt-provider — https://github.com/CalCOFI/workflows/pull/new/fix-cruise-key-and-mt-provider
    - ingest_calcofi_bottle.qmd: drop cruise_key_0 from casts.parquet, derive provider/dataset from _source_datasets for measurement_type, re-put_gcs_file casts after the bridge fixes it
    - metadata/swfsc/ichthyo/flds_redefine.csv: the taxon/tsn upstream rename
    - metadata/swfsc/ichthyo/{flds,tbls}_raw.csv: regenerated source snapshots
  - query fix-cruise-key-0 — https://github.com/CalCOFI/query/pull/new/fix-cruise-key-0
    - _queries/{datasets/bottle, spatial/bbox, temporal/date-range}.md: cruise_key_0 → cruise_key

  Next: the query app's bump-default-version.yml cron will detect latest.txt = v2026.05.20 within 6h and bump _config.yml:default_version, which Pages will deploy. You can also trigger it manually via gh 
  workflow run -R CalCOFI/query bump-default-version.yml if you want it immediately.

But then why after updating to the latest version and running the tests does the query I previously shared fail? https://calcofi.io/query/#browse--cruises
  ✗ Query failed: Binder Error: Referenced column "cruise_key" not found in FROM clause! Candidate bindings: "read_parquet.cruise_key_0", "read_parquet.site_key", "read_parquet.cast_key",
  "read_parquet.grid_key", "read_parquet._source_file" LINE 10: GROUP BY cruise_key ^

⏺ Pushed. Pages will rebuild in ~1 min. After that, every query form (cruises, bbox, date-range, datasets/*, etc.) will default to whatever _config.yml:default_version says — currently v2026.05.20.
  
  The fix chain is now:
  1. release_database.qmd uploads release to GCS
  2. test_release.qmd runs all queries; on full pass, writes latest.txt
  3. bump-default-version.yml polls latest.txt every 6h, bumps _config.yml:default_version on a commit
  4. Pages rebuilds, every form's version field defaults to the new value (via form-field.html override)
  5. Browser users get the current release without staleness
  
  Hard refresh https://calcofi.io/query/#browse--cruises once Pages finishes (~1 min) and the form should pre-fill v2026.05.20. The cruise_key_0 error will still happen until the fix-cruise-key-0 PR is merged
   though — that's the separate fix to the three queries that reference the old column name.

Ok, merged fix-cruise-key-0, but now seeing v2026.05.14 parquet files in SQL despite default v2026.05.20 showing [Image #2]

⏺ Two queries have hardcoded URLs in their textarea defaults. I'll introduce a __VERSION__ placeholder that the form-field include substitutes with site.default_version, then swap the literals.

⏺ Pushed (after fast-forwarding to pick up the merged fix-cruise-key-0 PR). Pages will rebuild in ~1 min.

  After it deploys, every textarea default that previously embedded releases/v2026.05.14/ will render with releases/v2026.05.20/ instead — no per-query YAML edits needed on future bumps. The
  bump-default-version.yml cron writes _config.yml:default_version, and form-field.html's two-step substitution propagates that everywhere:

  1. def = def | replace: "__VERSION__", site.default_version — for any field whose YAML default embeds the placeholder
  2. def = site.default_version (special-case for name == "version") — for the version field itself

  Hard refresh https://calcofi.io/query/#bio-env-matching--custom once Pages finishes and the textareas should pre-fill with v2026.05.20 URLs.

## 2026-05-20 calcofi4r: cleanup drift README.md vs README.Rmd

Ok, clean up the drift between Heads-up: there's a long-standing drift between calcofi4r/README.Rmd (sparse) and README.md (rich, hand-written). I appended See-also to both, but did NOT regenerate          
  README.md from README.Rmd (would have                                                                                                                                                                         
    wiped the rich content). Worth either porting README.md's content back into README.Rmd, or stopping treating README.Rmd as the source. Out of scope for this PR.

  What's now true:
  - README.Rmd is the canonical source with the full content (177 lines + See-also)
  - All example chunks are eval = FALSE — devtools::build_readme() is safe to run without GCS access or API keys
  - README.md is freshly regenerated from README.Rmd and they no longer drift
  - Bumped example version to v2026.05.19 and added metadata.json to the documented release directory layout

  Future maintenance: edit README.Rmd, run devtools::build_readme(), commit both files. The pre-existing trap (silent README.md wipe) is closed.

## 2026-05-19.d `query`: update with schema

Now that we have `metadata.json` in the GCS, devise a practical intuitive way to show the schema in the `@../query` app. We previously created `calcofi4r::cc_erd()` to generate a Mermaid diagram and then render that as an image in a QMD for zoom/pan with Lightbox, but with a pure HTML/JS approach there is probably a more direct and prettier way to do that. We would also want the full set of tables with descriptions and columns with data type, description and units a la `calcofi4r::cc_db_catalog()`. This would help inform the pre-baked queries and especially for any custom query building.

I am also unclear how to proceed with a new database release per running `@_targets.R`. All queries should probably be tested in this repo and the default db version updated in the repo and then website updated via GH Action. 

←  ☐ Scope  ☐ ERD approach  ☐ UI placement  ☐ Release flow  ✔ Submit  →

**Scope** These are two related but distinct workstreams (schema browser UI + release/test/deploy loop). Should I plan both in one file or split them?

❯ 1. Both in one plan
     Single plan covers schema browser UI, query testing, default-version bump, and GH Action. Larger but coherent.
  2. Schema browser only
     Focus this plan on the schema browser UI; release workflow handled separately later.
  3. Release workflow only
     Focus this plan on _targets.R → test → bump → deploy. Schema browser handled separately.

**ERD approach** How should the ERD diagram render in the query app?

❯ 1. Mermaid.js client-side (recommended)
     Reuse cc_erd() to emit Mermaid syntax into metadata.json; render in-browser with mermaid.js + svg-pan-zoom. Pretty, zoom/pan native, no new R code needed.
  2. Interactive SVG (D3 / dagre-d3)
     Generate a custom force-directed or layered SVG from relationships.json client-side. Most flexible (click table → highlight FKs, drag nodes) but heaviest lift.
  3. Static SVG/PNG uploaded with release
     cc_erd() → SVG/PNG uploaded to GCS alongside metadata.json; embed with svg-pan-zoom. Simplest, same as today but interactive.
  4. Reusing `cc_erd()` to produce a mermaid ERD diagram would probably best be done 
     in `@release_database.qmd` and pushed to the GCS as another sidecar companion to
     `relationships.json`

**UI placement** Where should the schema browser live in the query app?

❯ 1. New "Schema" sidebar category
     Add to the existing left-nav accordion alongside Browse / Custom. Lowest friction, matches existing pattern. Sub-items: ERD, Tables, Columns, Datasets.
  2. Dedicated /schema page + sidebar link
     Full-width page (Jekyll-rendered or JS) with tabs: ERD / Tables / Columns / Datasets. Linked from sidebar and intro. Most room to breathe.
  3. Header button → modal/drawer overlay
     "Schema" button next to theme toggle opens an overlay. Always accessible while writing a query, but more JS to wire up.
  4. Ok, I'm realizing this will be a useful reference more generally, so let's make it standalone. I created Github repo `@../schema` (`git clone https://github.com/CalCOFI/schema.git`) so it will be live at https://calcofi.io/schema. Ideally a dropdown will be available to view any past schemas (and descriptions) too. For the `@../query`, can you enable the sidebar to be collapsable?

**Release flow** How should new releases get promoted to the live query app?

❯ 1. Gate latest.txt on tests passing (recommended)
     release_database.qmd stops auto-pushing latest.txt. A new test step in workflows runs queries; if green, it pushes latest.txt AND opens a PR in query repo bumping _config.yml. GH Action merges/deploys.
  2. Keep auto-latest.txt; add separate "promote" target
     release_database.qmd keeps pushing latest.txt (so calcofi4r users always get latest). A separate target/notebook tests queries and, if green, opens the query-app PR. Decouples R-package latest from 
     website default.
  3. Single pinned file in workflows repo
     Add e.g. metadata/current_release.txt to workflows repo. _targets.R updates it only after tests pass. GH Action watches this file and mirrors it to query app + GCS latest.txt.

  Workflows repo (/Users/bbest/Github/CalCOFI/workflows)

  - Reads the just-built release version from data/releases/
  - Discovers every _queries/**/*.md in the sibling CalCOFI/query repo

  - Skips sql_builder: queries (JS-only)

  - Renders a pass/fail/skip DT table; writes test_results.json to GCS
  - Writes latest.txt only if zero failures; otherwise stop()s so the target fails

## 2026-05-19.c api-h3t in Python

The `@../api-h3t` (used by `@../int-app`) is using `sqlglot` to verify valid SQL in Python, but implemented as an R Plumber service. Could this be much faster if rewritten in Python using FastAPI or similar?

⏺ Here's the short version, then a couple of clarifying questions before I'd recommend committing to a rewrite plan.

  TL;DR: No, it would not be much faster per request. The bottleneck is DuckDB (10–1000 ms), not Plumber or the reticulate→sqlglot bridge (which loads Python once at startup and is ~1–5 ms per call).
  Varnish in front means most requests are <100 ms cache hits regardless of language.

  Where Python/FastAPI would genuinely help:
  - Concurrency. Plumber serializes requests per worker; FastAPI/uvicorn handles concurrent tile requests in parallel against DuckDB's thread-safe read connection. Real win on cache-miss bursts (new filter
  → N tiles fetched simultaneously).
  - Simplicity. No reticulate, no dual R+Python runtime in the Docker image. ~380 LOC R → ~200–250 LOC Python. The sql_validate.py already exists as native Python.
  - Not a perf fix — the int-app agent found no documented slowness complaints; maps.qmd reports the current setup as fast.
  
  A couple of questions before I commit a real implementation plan.

←  ☐ Motivation  ☐ Scope  ✔ Submit  →
  
**Motivation** What's the underlying motivation for considering this rewrite?

  1. Observed slowness / users notice lag
     There's a real perf complaint driving this — likely burst-load on filter changes or cache misses.
  2. Architectural fit (R↔Python bridge feels off)
     Code/ops simplicity is the goal; perf is fine but the polyglot setup is awkward to maintain.
❯ 3. Future workload concerns
     Current load is fine but you expect more users / heavier queries and want headroom before pain shows up.

**Scope** If we proceed, what's the desired scope?

❯ 1. Full rewrite plan in FastAPI + parity tests
     I'll write a detailed plan for a FastAPI port with deploy strategy and ETag/cache parity verification.
  2. Stop here — assessment is enough
     You wanted the analysis, not an implementation. No plan beyond this.
  3. Benchmark first, decide later
     Plan a small load test against the current api-h3t (concurrent cache-miss tile requests) to confirm whether concurrency is actually a bottleneck before any rewrite.

PLAN: ./libs/plans/**2026-05-19 Rewrite `api-h3t` from R Plumber to Python FastAPI.md**

I am also interested in using this service with different databases -- can that be another parameter with a default db if not mentioned for backward compatability? Does Varnish and/or FastAPI send to the client zipped if it can handle it for improved performance?

←  ☐ Location  ☐ Scope  ✔ Submit  →

     Clean git history, separate CI, clearer cutover. I'll create /Users/bbest/Github/CalCOFI/api-h3t-py/ locally; you can `gh repo create` later.
  2. Subdirectory: api-h3t/py/
     Both implementations in one repo during transition. Easier side-by-side parity testing; messier git history.

     Use a `python-rewrite` branch in the existing api-h3t repo; one repo, isolated from main.
❯ 4. Please proceed with populating into `@../api-h3t-py`, created from:
```bash

git clone https://github.com/CalCOFI/api-h3t-py.git
```

**Scope** What's the scope for this session?

     Project structure, copy sql_validate.py, port h3t_query.R to Python, golden-output parity tests. Foundational, low-risk.
❯ 2. Everything in this session

⏺ Done. Here's the final summary.

  What was built

  Repo: /Users/bbest/Github/CalCOFI/api-h3t-py/ (cloned from CalCOFI/api-h3t-py)

  Source (app/):
  - sql_validate.py — verbatim copy of the existing native-Python validator
  - h3t_query.py — port of h3t_query.R (zoom→res, bbox, SQL wrappers)
  - config.py — env parsing with multi-DB registry + legacy DUCKDB_PATH fallback
  - db.py — DuckDB connection registry (one shared con per DB, per-request cursors with statement_timeout)
  - tiles.py — base64 decode, {{res}} substitution, ETag, cache headers, cell payload
  - main.py — FastAPI app: /health, /h3t/{z}/{x}/{y}.h3t, /stats, /meta with ?db= support, async run_in_threadpool for concurrent DuckDB calls, CORS middleware, optional gzip middleware
  
  Tests (tests/): 67 tests, all passing — test_config.py, test_sql_validate.py, test_h3t_query.py (parity vs R-generated fixtures/r_golden.json), test_tiles.py, test_endpoints.py (FastAPI TestClient with
  mocked DB layer)

  Tooling: Dockerfile (python:3.13-slim), pyproject.toml, requirements.txt, requirements-dev.txt, scripts/generate_r_golden.R, .gitignore, .dockerignore, full README.md

  End-to-end verification

  - Server boots against real DuckDB; /health and /meta return valid responses
  - Invalid SQL → 400 with reason from sqlglot
  - Unknown DB → 400 with allowed list
  - CORS preflight returns proper headers

  What's left (per plan)

  - Run live parity tests against the R service with real queries
  - Update api-h3t/plumber.R:155, :192 to the new stable-string ETag scheme
  - Load test (vegeta/oha) to confirm concurrency win on cache-miss bursts
  - Varnish backend cutover

⏺ All pushed. Three commits live:
  - CalCOFI/api-h3t-py @ b880c99 — scaffold
  - CalCOFI/api-h3t-py @ 3aa6deb — deploy.md
  - CalCOFI/api-h3t @ f590b34 — stable-string ETag
  
  Full deployment instructions are in api-h3t-py/deploy.md. Here's the condensed version:

  Cutover at a glance

  Strategy: parallel deploy → parity test → flip Varnish backend → flush cache. The R container stays running for instant rollback.

  1. On the server, pull the new code


  sudo -u bebest git -C /share/github/CalCOFI clone https://github.com/CalCOFI/api-h3t-py.git
  git -C /share/github/CalCOFI/api-h3t pull --ff-only   # picks up the ETag change

  2. Add h3t_api_py to server/docker-compose.yml alongside h3t_api

  Same env (DUCKDB_PATH, port 8889) plus H3T_DB_NAME=default and H3T_APP_GZIP=false. Both containers can share port 8889 internally — docker resolves them by hostname (h3t_api vs h3t_api_py).

  3. Build & launch

  cd /share/github/CalCOFI/server
  sudo docker compose build h3t_api_py
  sudo docker compose up -d h3t_api_py
  sudo docker compose logs -f h3t_api_py   # wait for "Uvicorn running"

  4. Parity check from the docker network

  sudo docker run --rm --network=server_default -v "$PWD:/work" -w /work \
    python:3.13-slim sh -c "pip install -q httpx && python \
    /share/github/CalCOFI/api-h3t-py/scripts/parity_check.py \
    --r-base http://h3t_api:8889 --py-base http://h3t_api_py:8889 \
    --release v2026.04.08"
  Must print OK: all checks passed before proceeding.

  5. Flip Varnish backend

  Edit server/varnish/default.vcl: change .host = "h3t_api" → .host = "h3t_api_py". Then:
  sudo docker compose exec varnish varnishadm vcl.load v_py /etc/varnish/default.vcl
  sudo docker compose exec varnish varnishadm vcl.use v_py
  sudo docker compose exec varnish varnishadm ban 'req.url ~ "^/h3t/"'   # flush
  Zero downtime — vcl.use is hot-swap.

  6. Verify through Caddy

  curl -s https://h3t.calcofi.io/h3t/health | jq .
  curl -sI "https://h3t.calcofi.io/h3t/4/3/6.h3t?q=$Q&release=v2026.04.08" \
    | grep -Ei '^(HTTP|X-Cache|ETag|Cache-Control)'
  Open int-app, pan/zoom, watch network panel for X-Cache: HIT.

  7. Rollback (if needed)

  Edit VCL back to .host = "h3t_api", reload — the R container is still running.

  8. Decommission after ~1 week

  docker compose stop h3t_api && docker compose rm -f h3t_api, remove block from compose file, commit.

  Breaking-change audit

  Three response shapes are different — int-app doesn't touch any of them, but other clients should be checked:
  - /h3t/health body — {db, db_mtime} → {default_db, dbs:{name:{path,mtime}}}
  - /h3t/meta body — adds available_dbs, default_db, db, db_mtime
  - Adds ?db= query param (optional; default preserves behavior)

```bash
claude --resume 02a3e2a1-2bfb-48ec-b648-94c9bafca234
```

## 2026-05-19.b add table/column metadata to DuckLake

The initial workflows in `@_targets.R` have descriptions as markdown of tables and for columns also units under `@metadata/` which somehow got dropped from inclusion in the development of the parquet files uploaded into GCS for the integrated DuckLake database. There are vestigial functions in `@../calcofi4r/` that fetched this metadata from the Postgres database in `cc_describe_table()`, and from the API `@../api/plumber.R` connecting to the Postgres db with `cc_db_catalog()` and described in `@../docs` (eg https://calcofi.io/docs/db.html#metadata-and-documentation). There is a LOT of inconsistency with the vestigial Postgres across the CalCOFI repos and the entire lack of table and column documentation in the current DuckLake implementation. A new sidecar file per db release `metadata.json` could take on this role, or one of the existing ones (`catalog.json`, `relationships.json`) could be expanded upon. The skills `@./claude/skills` would need to also include explicit instructions.

←  ☐ Sidecar approach  ☐ Scope  ☐ Cross-cut tables  ✔ Submit  →

**Sidecar approach** Where should table/column descriptions and units live in the release?

❯ 1. New metadata.json sidecar    ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  2. Expand catalog.json          │ data/releases/v2026.05.14/                                                    │
  3. Expand relationships.json    │   catalog.json        # structural manifest (rows, partitioned)               │
                                  │   relationships.json  # PKs + FKs                                             │
                                  │   metadata.json       # NEW — tables{} + columns{} with description_md, units │
                                  │   RELEASE_NOTES.md                                                            │
                                  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

**Scope** How far should the plan reach beyond producing the sidecar?

  1. [ ] Update calcofi4r consumers
  Rewrite cc_describe_table() to JOIN DuckDB schema with the new sidecar (descriptions + units). Either deprecate cc_db_catalog() loudly or rewire it to consume the sidecar.
  2. [ ] Update docs/db.qmd
  Replace the 'Metadata and documentation' section (lines 256-310) that still describes the Postgres COMMENT pattern with the new sidecar-based pattern.
  3. [ ] Update skills
  Update generate-metadata, ingest-new, validate-ingest skills so the description/units columns of flds_redefine.csv and tbls_redefine.csv are actively populated (today they ship mostly empty for dic, 
  casts, summary tables).
  4. [ ] Fix catalog.json bugs
  Fix total_size: 0 hardcode (release_database.qmd:687) by summing parquet file sizes. Quick fix while we're touching that chunk.
❯ 5. [✔] All of the above 

**Cross-cut tables** How should release-only tables (no per-ingest source) get descriptions?

❯ 1. Add to metadata/dataset.csv-style registry
     Add a workflows/metadata/release_tables.csv that holds descriptions/units for tables built in release_database.qmd itself: cruise_summary, _spatial, _spatial_attr, taxon, taxa_rank, lookup, ship, 
     cruise. Loaded and merged at release time.
  2. Inline in release_database.qmd chunks
     Wherever the release notebook builds these tables, attach inline description literals (R named list) that get merged into the release metadata.json.
  3. Defer — descriptions empty for now
     Ship release metadata.json with descriptions only for tables that come from ingest notebooks; leave release-only tables with empty description_md until later.

**PLAN**: `@./libs/plans/2026-05-19 Release-level metadata.json sidecar for table/column descriptions and units.md`

  per-ingest metadata.json files — 22 tables, 236 columns, 5 datasets, 105 measurement types.
  - `calcofi4r`: `cc_describe_table()` left-joins DuckDB information_schema.columns with the release metadata.json (adds name_long, units, description_md, table-level description via attribute). `cc_db_catalog()`
   rewired off the dead api.calcofi.io endpoints to the sidecar; new version arg.
  - `docs`: `db.qmd` metadata section rewritten — drops Postgres COMMENT / pg_description / pgadmin.calcofi.io references; documents the per-ingest → release flow and consumer entry points.
  - `workflows`:
    - `release_database.qmd` now merges per-ingest `metadata.json` files plus `metadata/release_tables.csv` + `metadata/release_columns.csv` and uploads `metadata.json` next to `catalog.json`/`relationships.json`. Fixed
  total_size: 0 by summing GCS bytes via `gcloud storage du --summarize`.
    - New `metadata/release_tables.csv` and `metadata/release_columns.csv` with descriptions/units for `cruise_summary`, `_spatial`, `_spatial_attr`, `ctd_cast`, `ctd_thin`, `ctd_summary`.
    - Skills: `generate-metadata` (correct CSV schema docs, require descriptions/units, hand-off completeness check), `ingest-new` (post-render scan), `validate-ingest` (metadata.json hard assertions),
  `publish-template` (release-sidecar smoke test).

  **Open follow-ups** (not in this PR set):
  - `data/parquet/calcofi_ctd-cast/metadata.json` is missing — the per-ingest sidecar isn't being written for CTD. Until that's fixed, CTD descriptions come from the release_columns.csv I added; the proper fix is in `ingest_calcofi_ctd-cast.qmd`.
  - Open the four PRs on GitHub when you're ready — the push output gave you the URLs.


Fix `@ingest_calcofi_ctd-cast.qmd` to properly produce a `@data/parquet/calcofi_ctd-cast/metadata.json` as it should based on metadata in `@metadata/calcofi/ctd-cast`, similar to other workflows listed in `@_targets.R`. If metadata still missing, try to glean from source like `@~/My Drive/projects/calcofi/data-public/calcofi/ctd-cast/download/20-2105SH_CTDFinalQC/CTD-CSV-Format.pdf` and populate with likely / most reasonable values otherwise.
  
⏺ Done. CTD metadata is fully wired:

  What was broken:
  - The write_metadata chunk in ingest_calcofi_ctd-cast.qmd did call build_metadata_json(), but the working DB had cross-cutting tables (cruise, ctd_cast_derived) leaking in because no tables= filter was
  set. The notebook also wasn't re-rendered after the chunk was added, so the file simply didn't exist on disk.
  - Even when generated, ~140 columns shipped with empty descriptions because metadata_derived.csv missed key ctd_cast columns and didn't cover ctd_wide at all.
  - release_tables.csv had stand-in CTD rows from my earlier PR that were overriding the richer per-ingest descriptions once we filled them in. 

  What changed:

  1. ingest_calcofi_ctd-cast.qmd — build_metadata_json() call now passes tables = parquet_stats$table and workflow_url = .... Future renders produce a clean 6-table sidecar.
  2. metadata/calcofi/ctd-cast/metadata_derived.csv — added 9 ctd_cast columns + 79 ctd_wide columns + 2 cleanups (ctd_measurement.cruise_key, measurement_type.is_canonical). Descriptions/units for ctd_wide
   come straight from the CalCOFI CTD+Bottle CSV column format PDF you pointed me at (Sep 2015 spec — Temp1/2, Salt1/2_Corr, Ox1/2_CruiseCorr/StaCorr, ISUS/EstNO3, BAT/XMiss, PAR/SPAR, bottle measurements,
  all with proper units).
  3. calcofi4db::merge_metadata_json() — overlay semantics fixed: release_tables.csv / release_columns.csv now only fill missing fields, so per-ingest sources always win when both are present.
  4. metadata/release_tables.csv, release_columns.csv — dropped the redundant CTD rows; now only cover truly release-only tables (cruise_summary, _spatial, _spatial_attr).
  5. data/parquet/calcofi_ctd-cast/metadata.json — generated locally and confirmed: 6 tables, 128 columns, 0 empty descriptions. (Local file, not committed — regenerated by the notebook.)

  Verified release merge picks up ctd_thin description as "adaptively-thinned ctd_measurement: single cast direction, canonical measurement types, ~10 m depth grid…" (the rich per-ingest text), not the
  one-liner that was previously overriding it.

  Both branches pushed: calcofi4db metadata-sidecar and workflows metadata-sidecar.

 Ok, I've merged all repos. Please cleanup local and remote branches metadata-sidecar and checkout and pull main locally for all repos. Do I next need to run `Rscript -e 'targets::tar_make()'` to push an updated release? 

 ⏺ Clean across all four repos.

  On the release question: yes, targets::tar_make() is the path, with a couple of things to know:

  1. CTD is excluded from tar_make() (see _targets.R:18), which is exactly what you want — the locally-generated data/parquet/calcofi_ctd-cast/metadata.json (6 tables, 128 columns, 0 empty descriptions)
  will be picked up as-is. CTD won't re-run for hours.
  2. Bottle / ichthyo / DIC ingests will re-run if targets sees a changed dependency (it tracks each .qmd and its declared inputs). The new release_database.qmd chunk doesn't touch their inputs, but the
  ingest qmds themselves haven't changed — tar_outdated() will tell you exactly what will run.
  3. release_database.qmd will definitely re-run (we modified it), creating data/releases/v2026.05.19/ (today's date) and uploading four sidecars to gs://calcofi-db/ducklake/releases/v2026.05.19/:
  catalog.json (with the real total_size), relationships.json, the new metadata.json (schema 1.1), and RELEASE_NOTES.md.
  4. Heads-up on completeness: the bottle/ichthyo/DIC per-ingest metadata.json files still have many empty description_md and units (we only backfilled CTD). The release sidecar will ship with those gaps.
  If you want them filled before publishing, run /validate-ingest against each provider/dataset first — it'll list the gaps in flds_redefine.csv / metadata_derived.csv.

  Suggested order:
  Rscript -e 'targets::tar_outdated()'   # preview what will run
  Rscript -e 'targets::tar_make()'       # run it
  
  If you just want the new sidecar published without touching ingests at all, you can render the release notebook directly: quarto render release_database.qmd.

## 2026-05-17 calcofi.io/query as JS WASM duckdb

The docs `@../docs/data-access.qmd`, `@../docs/helpers.qmd` and `@../calcofi4r/vignettes/bio-env-matching.Rmd` all lay out the case for using parquet files and DuckDB from anywhere, but the WASM example simply points to https://shell.duckdb.org/ without instructions. Can we create a JavaScript only page with WASM DuckDB connnectors and these same helper functions (without any R or Python dependencies) for selecting the function, entering input arguments and getting the results, a la the client-side version of the Plumber API `@../api`? And can we update the referenced documentation with more specifics and consistency across?

                                  │                                           │

                                  │                                           │

                                  │   the SQL-builder port of calcofi4r/R/mat │
                                  │   (reusable; can be imported by other app │
                                  └───────────────────────────────────────────┘

   JS                             │ docs/match/                               │
❯ 2. Standalone HTML app          │ ├── index.html         standalone page (f │
 3. Quarto .qmd with              │ ├── app.js             UI + reactive logi │
   Observable JS                  │ ├── match.js           SQL-builder port o │
                                  │ └── style.css                             │
